parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I used to breeze through unexpected changes without a second thought, but lately even the smallest shift in my routine leaves me completely rattled. I catch myself holding my breath over things that never used to bother me at all. My chest gets tight and my thoughts start spinning out of control before I can even catch myself. It is just a weird feeling to watch my own reactions spiral over absolutely nothing. I keep waiting for it to pass, but the tension just lingers in the background.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you're going through a really challenging time, and it's completely understandable to feel unsettled by these changes in your reactions. It can be disorienting and even a little scary when you notice your own mind and body responding in ways that feel unfamiliar or out of your control.

First, I want to acknowledge how self-aware you are—it takes a lot of strength to recognize and name what you're experiencing. Sometimes, our bodies and minds start signaling to us that something deeper might need attention, even if we can't pinpoint exactly what it is right away.

It might help to gently explore what’s changed recently—not just in your routine, but perhaps in your life overall. Stress, even if it’s subtle or accumulated over time, can sometimes manifest in physical and emotional ways like the tightness in your chest or racing thoughts. It’s also possible that your tolerance for uncertainty has shifted, which is a normal part of being human, especially during times of transition or prolonged stress.

If you feel comfortable, you could try grounding techniques when you notice that tension building—things like deep breathing, focusing on your senses, or even just pausing to name what you’re feeling without judgment. Sometimes, giving yourself permission to feel rattled can take some of the power out of the reaction.

And remember, it’s okay to seek support if this continues to weigh on you. Talking to someone you trust, or even a professional, can help you navigate these feelings and find strategies that work for you. You don’t have to just wait for it to pass on its own.

How you’re feeling matters, and taking small steps to care for yourself during this time is a sign of strength, not weakness.

turns.user_message: What really gets to me is how this reaction has become my new normal instead of just a passing phase. I used to just shake off that nervous energy, but now it builds up until I feel completely overwhelmed by my own heartbeat. Every little surprise feels like a warning sign that something is about to go wrong. I try to ground myself by taking deep breaths, but the panic just keeps creeping back in anyway. It is exhausting to constantly monitor my own body for signs of trouble.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you’re describing something that has moved beyond everyday stress into a more persistent and consuming experience. When anxiety shifts from a temporary reaction to a constant background state, it can feel like you’re living in a heightened state of alert—always waiting for the next wave of panic. That kind of vigilance is draining, both mentally and physically.

You mentioned trying deep breathing, which is a good tool, but sometimes when anxiety is this entrenched, it can feel like trying to put out a fire with a cup of water. It might help to explore other grounding techniques that engage your senses or your body in a different way—like focusing on physical objects around you, gentle movement, or even redirecting your attention to a specific task.

It’s also worth considering whether speaking with a mental health professional could provide you with more tailored strategies. They can help you understand what might be driving this shift from temporary nerves to constant overwhelm, and work with you to build a toolkit that feels sustainable.

You’re already aware of what’s happening—that’s a significant first step. This isn’t just “in your head”; it’s a real experience that many people go through, and it doesn’t have to be your permanent normal.

turns.user_message: I honestly do not recognize myself anymore because this constant state of alert has completely taken over my everyday life. I used to laugh at people who got worked up over minor details, but now I am the one trapped in that exact cycle. My mind races so fast that I feel like I am about to jump out of my own skin. Every single moment feels heavy and urgent, leaving me completely drained before the day even really starts. I just want to go back to feeling steady and calm without having to fight for it.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you’re carrying an immense weight right now, and I hear how exhausting and disorienting it feels to be in this constant state of alert. It’s especially hard when you notice yourself becoming the very thing you once didn’t understand—that shift can feel like losing a part of who you are.

What you’re describing—the racing thoughts, the heaviness, the feeling of being trapped in a cycle of urgency—sounds like anxiety has taken a powerful hold on your daily life. It’s not just “worrying about small things.” It’s your nervous system being stuck in overdrive, and that is physically, mentally, and emotionally draining.

You deserve to feel steady and calm without having to fight for it. That longing for peace is completely valid. It might help to remember that this state isn’t permanent, even though it feels all-consuming right now.

Would you be open to exploring some gentle steps toward finding relief? Sometimes, even small shifts—like grounding exercises, speaking with a therapist, or allowing yourself moments of intentional rest—can begin to create space between you and that feeling of urgency. You don’t have to navigate this alone.
